{"step_by_step_thinking": "The patient presents with symptoms and laboratory findings consistent with Cushing syndrome. Key features include progressive weakness, obesity, abdominal striae, hypertension, and hyperglycemia. Laboratory findings show elevated 24-hour urinary cortisol (470 µg, above the normal threshold of <300 µg), and serum cortisol of 30 µg/mL (which is abnormally high, as normal is 5-23 µg/dL). The serum ACTH level is 2 pg/mL, which is below the normal threshold (>5 pg/mL), suggesting an ACTH-independent cause. However, the 48-hour high-dose dexamethasone suppression test shows no suppression of cortisol, which is consistent with Cushing syndrome. Since ACTH is low, this points to an adrenal source of cortisol, not a pituitary adenoma. The next step in management for an ACTH-independent cause is to evaluate for adrenal adenoma. Therefore, MRI of the adrenal glands is the appropriate next step to localize the source of cortisol production. MRI of the chest (B) is not indicated for adrenal pathology. Low-dose dexamethasone suppression test (C) is not appropriate as the high-dose suppression test has already been performed and is positive. Inferior petrosal sinus sampling (D) is used to differentiate pituitary from ectopic ACTH sources and is not relevant here because ACTH is low. Thus, the best next step is MRI of the adrenal glands.", "answer_choice": "A"}
